source.title: Social place-cells in the bat hippocampus

confidence_rationale: Identifies a dedicated CA1 cell population that encodes the position of a conspecific in allocentric coordinates, distinguishable from cells encoding inanimate moving objects. Establishes social/conspecific position as a privileged map channel.
failure_signatures: Hippocampal CA1 encodes conspecific position with cells distinct from those encoding inanimate moving objects, so social tracking is not a byproduct of generic moving-object representation.
If REE assumed only self-position is map-tagged, this falsifies that assumption: another agent's position is also a privileged channel.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Dorsal CA1 in observer bats contains a subpopulation that represents the position of another bat in allocentric coordinates; about half of these cells also represent the observer's own position. The representation differs from that of inanimate moving objects.
mapping.ree_translation: Social/conspecific position is a third map-tagged channel beyond self-position. Architecturally, this argues that the hippocampal map can be augmented with channel-specific cells for any behaviourally relevant object class — and that 'social' qualifies as such a class with its own cell pool.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Bat is a less common model; whether the same cell-class structure exists in rodents and primates was unknown when this paper was published (Danjo 2018 confirms the rat case in observational T-maze). Allocentric encoding in bats may benefit from echolocation precision in ways that don't transfer to other species.
mapping.source_context: Egyptian fruit bats, observational learning task, single-unit recording in dorsal CA1 of observer bat tracking demonstrator bat.

confidence_components.notes: Science paper, Ulanovsky lab, careful controls including inanimate-object comparator. Transfer risk slightly elevated by bat-specific factors (echolocation, flight) but Danjo 2018 in rat replicates the core finding.
